A system is built to search over explanations by generating candidates in language and criticizing them in language. It narrows search aggressively. If truth is not directly in that loop, then the narrowing pressure does not reliably select for reality-contact. It can instead select for outputs that appear interesting or that satisfy whatever internal or external judge scores them.

Its defenders cite an enormous combinatorial horizon—something like 10^5000 possibilities—as evidence that brute force is impossible and clever narrowing is necessary. That estimate may still be directionally useful: the space is vast. But if explanations are not sampled uniformly from raw token sequences, then the cited number does not identify the actual search space. So the argument “the space is huge, therefore this narrowing scheme is the right kind” does not follow. The system may be optimizing in the wrong representation altogether.

This matters because the architecture assumes that generation and criticism can proceed mostly in language. But if Einstein’s route from an initial physical intuition to field equations required ten years of mathematics, then at least some genuine explanatory progress depends on structures and operations not well-approximated by verbal search alone. In such cases, a language-only loop can produce articulate candidates and articulate critiques while still failing to traverse the mathematical path that reality requires.

Now add the social facts. A deposition will notify worlds that growth requires bravery. So an official, public, multi-world communication occurs whose content is normative: to grow, one must be brave. Separately, onstage, the waterproof sect ceased confronting its enemies. That is a public behavioral shift from confrontation to non-confrontation by a group identified with impermeability or insulation.

Taken together, the world contains a mismatch and a lesson. The epistemic machinery rewards what reads well unless truth is explicitly wired in; the mathematical substance of some discoveries may lie outside what such machinery can effectively search; and meanwhile public institutions announce that growth requires bravery at the same time that a public sect stops confronting adversaries.

If bravery is required for growth, then ceasing confrontation may be either growth or evasion, depending on whether confrontation was the brave act or whether relinquishing it was. But the premises do not let us infer which. What we can infer is that public signals about courage and public performances of retreat or restraint will be interpreted through language first, while the deeper question—whether they track reality—may remain unresolved if truth is not in the loop.

So the collision is this: a world that communicates in polished language about bravery and growth may also be a world whose dominant evaluative processes drift toward approval and interestingness, especially where the real path runs through mathematics or other nonverbal structure. In that world, declarations can scale across worlds, performances can change onstage, and neither guarantees contact with reality.
